segments.text: [MUSIC PLAYING]
CAREN CHANG: Hey, everyone.

I'm Caren, and today I'm
joined by Taj and Sa-ryong

to talk about the
latest developments

for on-device generative
AI in Android.

In this talk, we'll
cover how to consider

use cases for on-device
AI in Android apps, Gemini

Nano, Google's most
efficient model that's

optimized for mobile devices and
built with privacy and safety

by design, a set of new gen
AI APIs powered by Gemini Nano

to help your app perform the
most common tasks, apps that

are already using Gemini
Nano in production,

and preview what we're
working on next to help bring
Gemini Nano to more developers.

Let's start by exploring
what type of use cases
are well-suited for AI.

Successful Gemini
usage typically
focuses on one of the following.

Content consumption,
creation, or classification.

Gen AI can help users consume
content from different sources

by aggregating, condensing, or
extracting important information

for example, providing a
summary of users' notifications,

summarizing contents
of an email thread,

highlighting main
points of a document,

or extracting information from
an image to be returned as text.

Next, let's consider gen AI
for content creation use cases.

For example, gen AI can
help users brainstorm ideas

or generate prompts at the
beginning of the content
creation process.

During the creation
process, gen AI

can offer writing assistance,
such as checking grammar
or rephrasing recommendations.

And before sharing
content, gen AI

can help suggest captions,
hashtags, or taglines.

Finally, gen AI can be used
to provide better content
classification experiences.

For example, by analyzing
user generated content,

such as comments and
reviews, to filter for spam

or detect overall sentiment,
or categorizing content

into different
topics and themes,

such as organizing personal
notes or documents.

With these use cases
in mind, let's now

consider how on-device
gen AI is well
suited to perform these tasks.

On-device gen AI
refers to the ability

to process prompts and
data directly on the device

without sending
data to a server.

Since the server
is not required,
this offers a few advantages.

Sensitive user data can be
processed locally on the device.

Functionality of
the model does not

depend on internet
connectivity, meaning

the model can output
results, even with spotty
or no internet connection.

And lastly, there is
no additional cost

for each inference,
since everything
runs on the user's hardware.

With these advantages,
on-device gen AI

makes a great
solution for the use

cases involving content
consumption, creation,
and classification.

So let's dive into how
you can already enable
some of these use cases today.

Gemini Nano is Google's most
efficient large language model

for on-device tasks,
and you can now

build with Gemini Nano using
Android's AICore system service.
AICore is a system level module.

By using it to
access Gemini Nano,

apps can gain advantages
such as optimizations

based on device hardware to
enable low inference latency
and reduce power consumption.

No extra effort in managing
model deployment, including
updates to Gemini Nano model.
And local processing.

AICore can directly
access the internet.

Any requests to download
models or other information

are routed through
private compute service,

a separate companion
APK, AICore is also

designed to isolate
each inference request.

This means that requests
are handled independently

and processed from
one app at a time,

to mitigate the risk
of data being exposed
to other apps that use AICore.

Input data and resulting
outputs are never
stored anywhere on the device.

In addition, it's
important to ensure

that Gemini Nano
allows developers
to build with AI responsibly.

To achieve this, multiple
layers of protection

were implemented to limit
harmful or unintended results.

At its core, the
Gemini Nano model

is trained with
safety considerations.

On top of that, both the input
prompt and the results generated

by Gemini Nano
runtime are evaluated

against our safety filters
before providing results
to the app.

With these benefits
in mind, now let's

hear from Taj about how
you can easily adopt Gemini
Nano in your Android apps.

TAJ DARRA: We are
excited to announce

a new set of generative
AI APIs as part of ML Kit

to help your apps perform
on-device tasks using the Gemini
Nano model.

To start, we are releasing
four new APIs that
are built for on-device tasks.

Summarization, to summarize
texts such as chat conversations
and articles.

Proofreading, to help
users polish short content.

Rewrite, to assist users
in rewording content

in different styles,
such as formally,

casually, concisely,
or even emojify.

And image description, to
provide a short description
for your images.

Key benefits of
these APIs include

high level APIs that allow
for easy integration,

similar to the
existing ML Kit APIs.

Quality results out of the box,
meaning no extra effort required

for prompt engineering or fine
tuning for specific use cases.

Consistent quality across
supported devices and model
upgrades.

Now, let's dive into what
it means for these APIs
to be powered by Gemini Nano.

There are four main
components that make up
each of the gen AI APIs.

The first, the base
model, Gemini Nano.
That runs the inferences.

Next, for each task, we've
developed a low rank adaptation

or LoRA block to further improve
the quality of specific tasks.

Before each API's
release, we also

run a set of evaluation
metrics to standardize

the quality of the output across
different Android devices.

And lastly, each API is
built on optimized prompts

so that the model can return
the best quality results.

These components significantly
reduce developer effort

required to incorporate
gen AI in Android apps
with Gemini Nano.

Let's take a closer
look at what it

takes to implement one of
these APIs in an Android app.

For this example, let's try
summarizing a web article
to get the gist of it.

Start by defining which
task is being implemented.

In this case, it's
the summarizer task.

Then set different
options to guide the model

in processing the input request
and generating higher quality

results tailored to
the app's use case.

In this one, we want to create
a summarization of an article,

and we want the summarization
to be returned in a one bullet
format.

Next, check whether this API is
available on the current device

and whether it needs
to be downloaded.

If the feature is
available on the device,

start creating the
summarization request.

And if the feature hasn't
been downloaded yet,
start the download process.

Once the feature is
downloaded, run the inference.

And finally, once
output is ready,

read the results of
the summarization.

When the use case is completed,
be sure to release the resource.

Now, if we put it all together,
not including the code

to download the feature, it
took just 10 lines of code

to implement the summarization
feature with Nano.

This was the code example
for the summarization task.

But usage of all AI APIs will
follow this similar pattern

of defining the task to
implement, along with the input

and output format
desired, checking

whether the task is
available on-device

and whether it has
already been downloaded,

creating the request
to run the task,

and running the inference
to get a result output.

Here's how we best
use these gen AI APIs.

For the gen AI
summarization API,

consider using it for
summarizing call or message

transcripts that involve
two or more users,

and short articles or documents
less than 4,000 tokens long,
or about 3,000 English words.

For the gen AI proofreading
and rewrite APIs,

consider using it during
the content creation

process for short
content around 256 tokens

to help with tasks
like refining messages

in a particular tone, polishing
personal notes for easier

consumption later, and
rewording content to be

more suitable for
different settings.

And lastly, the
image description

API could be useful
in a lot of cases,

like generating titles
of images very quickly,

utilizing descriptions of
images for when you can't look

at your screen, like when
you're driving or listening

to a podcast, or generating
alternative text to help

visually impaired
users better understand
the content of their images.

For more detailed
documentation on all the gen

AI APIs, be sure to check out
the official documentation page
and sample on GitHub.

SA-RYONG KANG: Now
that you know how

to start using Gemini
Nano in your app,

let's take a look at some apps
that are already leveraging
Gemini Nano's capabilities.

Envision is an app that
speaks out the visual world

to help visually impaired people
lead more independent lives.

A common use case for Envision
is take a picture of document
and have it read aloud.

To improve this user
journey even more,

Envision integrated gen
AI API summarization

feature so users can get a
concise and meaningful summary
of captured document.

This significantly enhances
the user experience

by allowing them
to quickly grasp

the main points of the
document and determine

if more detailed
reading is desired,
and saving them time and effort.

During the API
integration process,

Envision engineers found
that the gen AI summarization

API was easy to
use, and they were

able to build out this
new gen AI powered feature
in just a couple of hours.

Google Home's new
Status widget uses

Gemini Nano to provide status
updates about your home.

The Nano model analyzes complex
data, such as the device status,

camera, events, and activity
logs, and then synthesizes

this information into a
simple summary report.

The Google Home engineers
found that Gemini Nano

is excellent for handling
specialized tasks
and saving on server costs.

Several Google Apps, including
Messages, Recorder, Talkback,

and GBoard, have also been using
Gemini Nano for summarization,

rewriting, and image description
to improve the user experience.

In addition, your Pixel phones
that support Gemini Nano now
include these helpful features.

The Weather app now gives you
a quick, personalized forecast
summary.
Less scrolling, more knowing.

On-device scam detection is now
running in Messages and Phone

by Google Pixel Screenshots
apps analyze your screenshots

to suggest relevant
actions, such as navigating
to an address.

It also allows you to
easily query information

like package tracking
number or a Wi-Fi password.

And lastly, imagine having an
efficient notetaker assisting
on your calls.

That's what we've built
in Phone by Google.

It not only transcribes
and summarizes your calls,

but also creates action items or
appointments on your calendar.

With so many apps already using
Gemini Nano in production,

we are excited to continue
working on improving our Gemini
Nano and Gemini API offerings.

Gemini Nano is becoming
more and more capable.

We are adding even better
support for languages

like German, Japanese,
Korean, Spanish, and more.

And in addition to
image and text input,

we are also working
on supporting audio.

At this year's I/O, we showcased
our on-device innovation

by launching a new
Gemma on-device model.

We are excited to soon bring
similar advancements along

with only with Google feature
to Gemini Nano for Android.

And to make it even easier for
you to build with Gemini Nano,

we are introducing
a gen AI prompt

API designed so you can
focus on achieving results
through well-crafted prompts.

This will be available
later this year.

Let me introduce Kakao Mobility,
creator of the popular South
Korean rideshare app KakaoT.

Let's take a look at how
they use the prompt API

to improve a critical
user journey in their app.

In the KakaoT app, users
can place delivery orders

by sending a message
through chat.

However, Kakao
faced the challenge

of easily extracting phone
numbers and addresses
from delivery request messages.

With the power of
prompting, they

could quickly obtain the
required information.

Because Nano understands
the meaningful entities,

you don't need to teach it what
phone numbers or addresses look
like in the context of messages.

Also, you might notice that
there were multiple names

and phone numbers in the
input, but Nano understands
which one is for the recipient.

Next, the prompt API gives you
a full control of the output.

Kakao wanted to get the
result from JSON format.

Providing a schema or an example
of desired response format

was enough to get
the expected result.

With this new implementation
to easily extract

critical information needed
to process each delivery order

request, Kakao is
expecting to save
more than 40 seconds per order.

This feature is coming to
all KakaoT users this month.

Even further, having a
consistent cross-device
experience was key.

For this, Kakao built
a unified function

leveraging Gemini
Flash in the cloud

when local inference
couldn't ensure good quality.

With this hybrid
AI approach, they

are able to provide
consistent experiences
to all of their users.

We are focused on
enabling even more use

cases with Nano's multi-modality
by gathering feedback

from developers and
partnering with companies
like Kakao Mobility.

CAREN CHANG: We've covered
a lot in this session.

To quickly recap, the new gen
AI APIs powered by Gemini Nano

are available for you
to implement today.

This includes APIs for
summarization, proofreading,
rewrite, and image description.

We'll continue to work on
improving the developer

experience with Gemini Nano,
so be sure to keep an eye

on our official AI documentation
at developer.android.com/ai
for the latest updates.

And we're excited to
see what you will build
with our on-device offerings.
[MUSIC PLAYING]
